speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is Thursday, February 22, 1996. I'm with Anna Heilman. Her maiden name is Anna Wajcblum My is Iris Bier-Starosta. The interview is taking place in Ottawa, Ontario, Canada, in English. Can you please state your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Anna Heilman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna, A-N-N-A. Heilman, H-E-I-L-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Hanka, H-A-N-K-A, Wajcblum, W-A-J-C-B-L-U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had you been known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on December 1, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-R-S-A-W, Warsaw; Poland, capital P-O-L-A-N-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city did you grow up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Jakob.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wajcblum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rebecca Wajcblum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And brothers, sisters? What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the youngest of three daughters. My eldest sister name was Sabina. And my middle sister name was Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there anybody else living in your house as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my nanny, Isabela Szapowska, was living with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-S-A-B-E-L-A S-Z-A-P-O-W-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the cold winters. It was very cold in Warsaw, and my mother believed in fresh air. So I had to go out with my nanny every day for a fresh air ride on a sled. And remember that I was crying all the way going out, through there, and coming back. So this is what I remember from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a wonderful sense of humor. My father traveled a lot. He was a representative of the Polish Arts and Crafts. And because of that, he traveled a lot. And when he used to come back, he used to tell us all kinds of stories from his travels in a very humorous way. And, of course, the only time we were all together was at the meal time, at suppertime. And it was usually during the suppertime that my father was entertaining us with his stories. And invariably, my sister Ester, whom we called Estuscha, used to laugh so much that she used to spill whatever she was drinking on the tablecloth. And my mother was furious. So-- [LAUGHS] those one of the things I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother? Do you have any fond memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a very unusual woman. She was very beautiful, she was very elegant, and she was kind of a snobbish side as well. She-- she-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- 'pride' was a middle name, I would say. When we were crossing the street in Warsaw and there was a great deal of traffic, she wouldn't look left or right. She would cross right through, and the traffic had to stop for her. And I remember I used to die a thousand death. But this was the kind of woman she was. We always had help in the house, so I never knew my mother to do any household duties. And yet, the particular strength of hers came during the war when, after the bombing of Warsaw, we didn't have any electricity and heating or any water. Ordinarily, the apartment used to be heated by coal in a tile-covered kind of a covered fireplace. And during the war, my mother, with her own hands, took out the tile from this place and put in a wood-burning stove to heat the apartment. And this was the first time that I have seen my mother doing something manual. It was so completely out of character. And her strengths came also doing the war when, regardless of the conditions and circumstances, she was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very particular in the way we looked and dressed. And she used to sole our shoes with leather that was taken from my father's factory transmission belts. And she used to sew our clothes and make absolutely sure that we looked the way we are supposed to look, regardless of what was going on around us. It's the kind of person she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to be the youngest of three daughters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was spoiled rotten. I could do anything I wanted. It was really terrible. It didn't go well with my mother, who was a disciplinarian. She-- we couldn't get off the table after a meal unless we finished what was on our plate. And I remember I hated the porridge in the morning, and I couldn't get off the table, so my nanny used to help me by eating it all so that I can get up and go. Other than that, I was everybody's darling. So I could get away with murder, which I often did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing-- I was the youngest at home, and I was five years old, and I was bored stiff sitting and looking through the windows. So my mother persuaded our school, which was a private, all-girls school, to let me into grade one. Of course, I was much too young. And I was accepted conditionally, that if I can fit in socially and academically, I will stay. Well, I did. So I started my schooling when I was five years old. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a private school.

speaker: interviewer
text: A private Jewish school, a private--?

speaker: interviewee
text: A private Catholic school, lay Catholic school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were relations between Jewish students and non-Jewish students?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a very special private, progressive school. There was-- there was no difference. Relationship between Polish and Jewish students were the same as between the Gentile girls and Gentile girls. There was no difference whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family identify as been Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that we are Jewish, but the Jewishness of our family wasn't never a focus. We were rather an assimilated family-- traditional, however, because we did have sedar Pesach in our family. And my parents used to go to synagogue on the High Holy Days, Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. But other than that, there wasn't much focus on Judaism. I didn't really know very much about Zionism or the religious aspect of Judais. I remember during the war-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the bombing of Warsaw, we met for the first time our next-door neighbors. We met them in bomb shelters downstairs. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And one day, they invited me to their house. And it was very dark. So being a very polite and well-behaved girl, I immediately put on the light, and I was screamed at. You are not supposed to put the light on! And I went home crying. I didn't know what it was all about, just to tell you how far removed we were from-- I didn't know. Of course, it was all straightened out. But they happened to be an Orthodox family, who did not put on light on Shabbat. Must have been Friday night or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about being Polish? What did that mean for you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's really hard for me to tell. Remember that when the war broke out in 1939, I was 11 years old. I know that we used to get the Polish newspaper, but where exactly on the political spectrum my father belonged, I really couldn't tell you. I don't know. I know that as a result of our upbringing at school, I felt myself fiercely Polish patriotic. I-- I remember that when the Germans came to Poland, Warsaw was bombed out, and we had no water. So I used to go with Isabela to a place where there was main water. We had to take buckets and kettles to bring water into the house. And it so happened that I saw the first Germans, that they had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] camp kitchen. And while Isabela stood in the queue, I went off on a little hill. And I was standing there looking around. It was all different. And the young German soldier came to me. He was having his lunch. And he was cutting a piece of black bread-- we haven't seen bread for a while-- and gave it to me. And I turned my back to him and went back to join Isabela, not as a Jewish girl, but as a Polish girl who would not take bread from an enemy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back a bit, did your parents or did you know anything about the rise of-- of Hitler and Nazism? Was it ever discussed in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very much discussed. My older sister had a private tutor, who then became her husband eventually. He was a student at the Warsaw University.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mieczyslaw Zeielinski. You want me to spell it? [LAUGHS] It is M-I-E-C-Z-Y-S-L-A-W, capital Z-E-I-E-L-I-N-S-K-I. He was studying to be a lawyer. And of course, as a student at the university, there was a great deal of talk about Hitler and Germany and whether there will be a war or there won't be a war and what is happening. I think at that time-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] just prior to the war, the preoccupation was that German would probably declare war on Poland and the Poles were invincible, they are going to defend themselves. And, you know, he was not seen, at least in our eyes, as a big threat, Polish versus Germany. We did know, of course, that there were-- that there was prosecution and persecution of Jews in Germany. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because many of the Jews were thrown out of Germany and came into Warsaw, and we used to meet some of them who were immigrants from Germany. So we knew what was going on in terms of Jews, you know. And we knew Hitler's antisemitic remarks and persecution of the Jews. Of course, we knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there changes in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. At that time-- it depends when we're talking about. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We weren't aware-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of the war, of course, until there was mobilization in 1939 already. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The focus, as far as I was concerned, was really on the Polish invincibility. We had at that time at the head of the Polish government a Marshall Smigly-Rydz. And there were all kinds of songs linked with his name, that as long as he's the leader of Poland, nothing is going to happen to Poland and so on and so forth. So it was more in that sense that we were aware. If there were any changes? Not from the Jewish point of view, I don't think. What about anti-Semitic incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I was aware as a child. I am aware now, but I wasn't then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when the Nazis invaded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as you know from history, Poland was the only country that stood against Nazis for about two, three weeks, I believe. And during that time, we were in Warsaw, and Warsaw was quite destroyed by the bombing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very frightening. We used to go to the makeshift bomb shelters, which were in the cellars under the buildings. Ordinarily, those cellars would contain potatoes and coal for the winter. They were huge. They weren't clean, so we used to go there when the alerts-- the sirens of the impending bombing used to come. We used to run to those shelters, and were always covered with dust of coal, because it was there still from the coal. On the one hand, this was my opportunity for the first time to meet our neighbors. On the other hand, we were sitting there, and we used to listen to the airplanes going by. And they were always experts, and they knew, those are ours, those are theirs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How could they tell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How could they tell?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. You know, they-- obviously they were, if I remember correctly, the heavy Messerschmitts that were the bombers and others that I don't remember. But they were always experts. They could tell by the sound of the plane what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But more frightening was when you could hear the heavy-- the hiss of a heavy bomb coming down. And-- and you were just sitting there and listening. Is it going to fall here? Are we going to be buried alive here? What's happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- boom! And the whole building, the cellar, would shake. All there was is very specific hiss of a Molotov cocktail that was going, like-- shh-- hiss, like this. And they were exploding with like a champagne bottle's corks. And they had phosphorus things, and they would light on contact. We didn't see it when we were in the cellar, but when we came out, we could see things burning through the contact. And it was very frightening. And I remember from time to time in the-- when there was no bombing. Incidentally, the sirens were not necessarily accurate, you know. They would start when the bombing already started, or they would start when the bombing didn't start. It was frightening. We lived on the third story of a three-story building. And one day, my mother and I went up to the apartment to water the plants and to get washed a little bit. And as we were coming down, on the second floor approximately, there was a big bang, and the whole house shook. We ran to the shelter, and when we came back, we found out that the shrapnel fell into our apartment and made a big hole in the balcony door. And the whole balcony fell through, right through to the first floor. In other words, if we would have been in the apartment a little bit longer, it probably would hit us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is what it was like. And then one day, the building across our street was bombed, and it was just horrible. Half of the building was-- fell down. And the rest that was standing just showed what used to be other people's apartments. You could see bus stops hanging in the air and wallpaper, shreds of wallpaper, even pictures. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was just across the street. The building, was which I lived all my life, was suddenly gone. Another thing I remember on the street-- there was a dead horse that must have been hit by flying shrapnel. It was-- it was terrible-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and very frightening.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: From time to time, there were bakeries. And I think that Isabela or my father-- somebody used to go to these bakeries and bring bread. Other than that, we knew that the war was coming. So we had some stored food, like rice and flour. And my mother used to bake bread from the stores that we had. I remember also that we had stores of sardines and chocolate. The rest we didn't have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father still able to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not during the bombing, no no. No. You know, nobody was able to work. Everything was disrupted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was about two weeks-- two, three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, as I told you before, you know, the Germans came. Warsaw was without electricity, without water. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It was still September. We went back to school. My oldest sister was already working. My-- Estuscha and I went to school. We went for a very short while. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Then the Germans-- they created the-- Jewish student can no longer attend public schools. And we were told by our teachers, who were very sympathetic, that we can no longer go to school. So we went home. At that time also, my sister's tutor, whom we called Mietec, who was in the Polish army-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] contacted my sister. And they decided that he has to run away for Poland, not as Jew at this time, but as a Polish officer who deserted-- not deserted, but who had to run away, you know, not to fall into the German hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister and my not-yet brother-in-law left for four East Poland at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were left, my parents, my sister, Estuscha, and I, at home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, because there was no electricity and there was no water, the factory was closed. I don't know if I mentioned to you that my father had a factory of hand-carved wooden objects. And he couldn't-- the factory was closed as soon as the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was closed by--

speaker: interviewee
text: By my father. It could no longer function. What my father did, he took-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wooden post cards from the First World War. And he used to have a little tray and sell it in the cafes where the Germans were sitting to get some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever have plans to escape, to go to East Poland or somewhere else-- with all of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at this point. My brother-in-law came back from East Poland, and he was trying to talk my parents into leaving with him to East Poland, telling them that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Telling them what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Telling them that the Germans are going to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] make great difficulties to the Jews, that we're not safe where we were. And my mother's response was that she lived through the first war, and she knows that surviving the war is hard, but after the war is even harder. So she wanted to stay where she was, to take care of our belongings and what have you, that the, quote, 'security' versus insecurity of going who knows where. So she was adamant she's not going anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like to say goodbye to your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was terrible. It was absolutely terrible. My-- my mother just found it very, very difficult. But then my sister was adamant, and they-- they did leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the rest of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stayed on, and-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] then, of course-- the Germans-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- the repercussions and persecution of Jews started at that time. At first-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course, we couldn't go to schools. Then there was a decree that the Jews-- I don't remember the exact order, but what happened was that the Jews could not live in certain parts of Warsaw. Warsaw was declared into the Aryan side and the Jewish side. So Jewish people who lived in the non-Jewish area had to move into the Jewish area. Then Jews--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a Jewish area all along, so luckily for us, we didn't have to move. But many people did have, and they usually had a very short notice about leaving what they had and move, not knowing where. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then the Jews were not allowed to have furs. And I remember going with my mother to a certain depot, where we had to leave our furs. And my sister and I had winter coats with fur collars. So my mother took off the collars, and we brought it all to the depot.
